{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Unsorted completion Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Unsorted completion It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Unsorted completion It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Unsorted completion {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Unsorted completion It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City) [[Category:Okitsu Shrine (Nihonmatsu)]][[Category:Completion tier 2]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg|250px]]
Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37|37|19.9|N|140|34|37.6|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |Alternate names = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima }} '''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|ja|二本松市}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山}}, considered a sacred mountain. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命}} The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}} == History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo Keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} and Buddhist practices.{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}} The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} shrine.{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}} == Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}} == Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[Natural Monument of Japan]].{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁才天}} * {{ill|Shugendo|ja|修験道}} == External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of
