revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659061]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg |location = [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37.622194444444446|N|140.57711111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |alternate_name = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu|WD=Q685491}} }}
'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[:en:Japan|Japanese]] [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]], a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine underwent [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]], separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[:en:Natural Monument of Japan|Natural Monument of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] * [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]] * [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]], a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine underwent [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]], separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[:en:Natural Monument of Japan|Natural Monument of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] * [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]] * [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[:en:Japan|Japanese]] [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]], a deity of wealth and prosperity. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>

'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[:en:Japan|Japanese]] [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]], a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine underwent [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]], separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[:en:Natural Monument of Japan|Natural Monument of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] * [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]] * [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659061]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg |location = [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37.622194444444446|N|140.57711111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |alternate_name = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu|WD=Q685491}} }}
'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[:en:Japan|Japanese]] [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]], a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine underwent [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]], separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[:en:Natural Monument of Japan|Natural Monument of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] * [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]] * [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[:en:Natural Monument of Japan|Natural Monument of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] * [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]] * [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659061]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg |location = [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37.622194444444446|N|140.57711111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |alternate_name = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu|WD=Q685491}} }}
'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[:en:Japan|Japanese]] [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]], a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine underwent [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]], separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[:en:Natural Monument of Japan|Natural Monument of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] * [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]] * [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima]], in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a [[:en:Japan|Japanese]] [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingo Keiun]] 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]], a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the shrine underwent [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]], separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a [[:en:Natural Monument of Japan|Natural Monument of Japan]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] * [[:en:Benzaiten|Benzaiten]] * [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]]
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659061]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|en|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|WD=Q685491|lt=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37.622194444444446|N|140.57711111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |alternate_name = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu|WD=Q685491}} }}
'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|en|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|WD=Q685491|lt=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japanese}} [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}} and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}}
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}} and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}}
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.

The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>

The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|en|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|WD=Q685491|lt=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37.622194444444446|N|140.57711111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |alternate_name = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu|WD=Q685491}} }}
'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|en|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|WD=Q685491|lt=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japanese}} [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}} and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}}
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}} and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}}
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japanese}} [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.

The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}} and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隠津島神社 (二本松市)]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Okitsushima Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:木幡山・隠津島神社.jpg |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|en|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|WD=Q685491|lt=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima}}, Kihata Oaza Jike 49, Fukushima Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|37.622194444444446|N|140.57711111111112|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} |Rank = Minor Shrine of the Engishiki, Former Prefectural Shrine |Foundation = {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE, traditional) |Main Hall = Three-bay Nagare-zukuri with copper roof |alternate_name = Kihata no Benten-sama (弁天様) |Main Festival = April 25 |Rituals = Kihata's Banner Festival (December, first Sunday) |Map = Japan Fukushima |location = {{ill|Nihonmatsu|WD=Q685491}} }}
'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|en|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|WD=Q685491|lt=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japanese}} [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Mount Kihata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}} and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo}}
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>
== History == Okitsushima Shrine is traditionally said to have been founded during {{ill|Jingo-keiun|en|Jingo-keiun|WD=Q1373049|lt=Jingo Keiun|1=Jingo-keiun}} 3 (769 CE) by {{ill|Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja|丈部継足|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasetsukabe no Tsugutari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the ancestor of the Abe family. It is believed to have originated as a sacred site on Mount Kihata, later becoming associated with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo|1=Shugendō}} and Buddhist practices.<ref name="Sato">{{Cite book |title=式内社調査報告 |author=Yoichi Sato |volume=14 |publisher=皇學館大学出版部 |year=1986}}</ref>
The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>
== Festivals == The shrine holds its main festival on April 25 and the Kihata's Banner Festival in December. The latter, a unique ritual involving vibrant banners, has been preserved as a critical cultural heritage event.<ref name="Festival">{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html |title=木幡の幡祭り |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref>
== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>
== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan|1=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo|1=Shugendō}}
== External Links == * [http://okitushima.com/ Official Website of Okitsushima Shrine] * [http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/kanko/kohata/top.html Kihata's Banner Festival] (Nihonmatsu City)

'''Okitsushima Shrine''' (隠津島神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|en|Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|WD=Q685491|lt=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima|1=Nihonmatsu, Fukushima}}, in the Kihata area. It is often referred to as "Kihata no Benten-sama" (木幡の弁天様) and is renowned for the annual Kihata's Banner Festival, designated as a {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japanese|1=Japan}} [[:en:Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]]. The shrine is situated on {{ill|Mount Kihata|ja|木幡山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Kihata|1=Q11518389|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518389}}, considered a sacred mountain.
== Deities == The shrine enshrines the {{ill|Munakata Sanjojin|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|1=Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=Three Munakata Goddesses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Sanjojin|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Munakata Goddesses|qq=}}: * {{ill|Okitsushima-hime|ja|隠津島姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okitsushima-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|田心姫命|1=Tagorihime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagirihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagorihime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タキリビメ}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫命|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=zh|5=多岐都比賣命|6=fr|7=Tagitsuhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
The name "Kihata" (蚕機) is thought to symbolize silkworm cultivation, associating the shrine with the deity of sericulture.<ref name="Kikuchi">{{Cite book |title=日本の神々 |author=Katsuhiko Kikuchi |publisher=Hakusuisha |year=1984}}</ref>

The shrine’s historical name, "Benten Shrine," reflects its association with {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}}, a deity of wealth and prosperity. During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the shrine underwent {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, separating its syncretic elements. It was renamed Okitsushima Shrine in 1902, aligning with its identification as a potential {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} shrine.<ref name="Fukushima">{{Cite book |title=福島県の地名 |publisher=Heibonsha |year=1993}}</ref>

== Architecture == The main hall, rebuilt in 1800 under the orders of the Nihonmatsu Domain, features a three-bay Nagare-zukuri style with a copper roof. The shrine complex also includes a historically significant {{ill|Three-storied pagoda|ja|三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|2=ja|3=三重塔|4=zh|5=三重塔|1=Three-storied pagoda|lt=Three-storied pagodas|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|13=User:Immanuelle/Three-storied pagoda|qq=}}, designated as a prefectural cultural property.<ref name="Architecture">{{Cite web |url=http://www4.pref.fukushima.jp/furusato-in/sub/shosai.php?data_id=601 |title=木幡山隠津島神社本殿、拝殿 |publisher=Fukushima Prefecture}}</ref>

It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan|1=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo|1=Shugendō}}

== Cultural Significance == The shrine encompasses Mount Kihata, a prefectural natural monument and health protection forest. It features notable relics, including a grand cedar designated a {{ill|Natural Monument of Japan|en|Natural Monument of Japan|WD=Q122904442|lt=Natural Monument of Japan|1=Natural Monument of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.city.nihonmatsu.lg.jp/history/bunkazai/kuni/100-290-001.html |title=木幡の大スギ |publisher=Nihonmatsu City}}</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|WD=Q818468|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|WD=Q1483875|lt=Shugendo|1=Shugendō}}
